title: 1 1 Partial Differentiation
body:
TOPIC 1 : FUNCTIONS OF SEVERAL VARIABLES
                    1.1 PARTIAL DIFFERENTIATION

                       The definition of partial differentiation:
           The partial derivative of z(x, y) with respect to x and y is defined as

  ∂z             z(x + ∆x, y) − z(x, y)           ∂z             z(x, y + ∆y) − z(x, y)
     = zx = lim                         ,            = zy = lim
  ∂x       ∆x−→0         ∆x                       ∂y       ∆y−→0          ∆y


In your previous studies, you have studied functions of a single variable y(x) and their rate
of change dy/dx. In Engineering and the Sciences, we often need to deal with complicated
systems that depend on many variables. In these lectures, we will introduce partial dif-
ferentiation, which extends the familiar rules of calculus for functions of a single variable
to functions of several variables.

For example, the function z(x, y) depends on two variables, x and y. We denote the rate
of change of z in the x and y directions as
                               ∂z                    ∂z
                                  = zx      and         = zy .
                               ∂x                    ∂y
The formal definition of these derivatives is given above but in practice all we need to
remember is that the partial derivative treats all other variables exactly as if
they were constant.
                     ∂z     ∂z

                                        3
                    Plotting functions of several variables
Recall that the graph of a function of one variable y = f (x) is a curve in two-dimensional
space R2 . The graph of a function of two variables z = f (x, y) is a surface in three-
dimensional space R3 .

Example 5 Sketch each of the following surfaces in space:

a) 3x + 4y + 6z = 12




b) x2 + y 2 + z 2 = 25

                                       SUMMARY
a) The co-ordinate axes x,y,z form a right-handed system.

b) ax + by + cz = d is a plane.

c) x2 + y 2 + z 2 = r2 is a sphere centred on the origin with radius r.

d) If a variable is missing then the function is independent of this variable. Extend the
      two dimensional curve to infinity in this direction.

                                         Higher order derivatives

As with ordinary derivatives, we can define second and higher order partial derivatives.

Example 7 If z = f (x, y) = x2 sin(y) + x3 y + y 5 find

∂x∂y    ∂x           ∂y




 ∂ 2z

        ∂            ∂z
      =                       = zxy =
∂y∂x    ∂y           ∂x




Notice that in the shorthand notation zxy , we first calculate the x derivative and then
calculate the y derivative. In this example, you will observe that the mixed derivatives zxy
and zyx give the same answer. This will generally be true for most well-behaved functions,
in which case it does not matter what order you take the derivatives. It is not
always true however!
